title: Changing a ballcock in a cistern
subtitle: Isolate, drain, unbolt the old valve, fit the new one
excerpt: How to replace a faulty float valve (ballcock) in a WC cistern or cold-water storage cistern: isolating the supply, draining the cistern, removing the old valve, fitting a modern diaphragm replacement, and setting the water level.
sourceNotes: Standard UK plumbing practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Water Regulations Advisory Scheme (WRAS) guidance on float valves. Torbeck and Fluidmaster installation instructions.

recipeTools.notes: For holding the valve body while tightening the backnut.

glossaryTerms.definition: A float-operated water inlet valve in a cistern, that closes when the float rises to the working level. Old-pattern brass and rubber-diaphragm types; modern Torbeck and Fluidmaster variants.

content.text: A faulty
ballcock

either lets the cistern overflow continuously or fails to fill. The repair is always a complete valve replacement rather than an attempt to overhaul the old diaphragm or ball assembly; modern Torbeck or Fluidmaster side-entry or bottom-entry valves are cheap, reliable, and fit the standard threaded inlet on any cistern.
Isolate and drain
Step 1, isolate the supply.

Turn the isolating valve on the supply pipe to the cistern a quarter turn so the slot is across the pipe. If there is no isolating valve, close the main household stopcock. Flush the WC to drain the cistern.
Removing the old valve
Step 2, disconnect the supply pipe.
Use the adjustable spanner to undo the supply pipe nut at the base of the valve, where the pipe enters the cistern. Have a cloth ready; residual water will drain.
Step 3, remove the backnut.
Inside the cistern, unscrew the large plastic or brass backnut that clamps the valve to the cistern wall. The old valve lifts out. Inspect the cistern inlet hole; it should be clean and undamaged.
Fitting the new valve
Step 4, fit the new valve.

Modern side-entry valves have a threaded shank that passes through the cistern inlet hole. Apply PTFE tape to the threaded portion that will project inside the cistern (the backnut threads onto this). Insert the valve from outside, fit the rubber washer and backnut on the inside, and hand-tighten. Tighten the backnut a further half turn with the adjustable spanner, holding the valve body steady so it points in the correct direction.
Step 5, reconnect the supply pipe.
Reconnect the supply pipe nut to the valve inlet, using PTFE tape on the thread. Hand-tight plus a quarter turn with the spanner.
Setting the water level
Step 6, restore supply and set the level.

Restore the water supply slowly and allow the cistern to fill. The correct water level is about 25 mm below the overflow pipe. On a Torbeck valve, adjust the water level by raising or lowering the float arm clip. On a Fluidmaster, turn the adjustment screw. Flush and watch the refill; the valve should close cleanly as the water reaches the set level.
Common mistakes
Cistern still overflows after fitting a new valve.
The float arm is set too high and the valve does not close before the water reaches the overflow. Lower the float arm or adjust the Fluidmaster screw anticlockwise to reduce the fill level.
Drip at the backnut inside the cistern.
The backnut is not tight enough, or the rubber washer is not seated flat. Tighten the backnut a further quarter turn.
